suggested_title: PAR-20-043 — Pathology: health, laboratory, research, approaches
synopsis: This application would address Cancer Center Support Grants (CCSGs) for NCI-designated Cancer Centers (P30 Clinical Trial Optional) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on health, laboratory, research, approaches and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in health, laboratory, research, approaches using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

suggested_title: PAR-20-043 — Pathology: cancer, which, research, support
synopsis: This application would address Cancer Center Support Grants (CCSGs) for NCI-designated Cancer Centers (P30 Clinical Trial Optional) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on cancer, which, research, support and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in cancer, which, research, support using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

synopsis: This application would address Resource-Related Research Projects for Development of Models and Related Materials for Studying Human Health and Diseases (R24 Clinical Trials Not Allowed) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on human, including, develop, health and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in human, including, develop, health using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

suggested_title: HQ003423NFOEASD01 — Pathology: two, apply, see, specific
synopsis: This application would address NDEP STEM Open NFO by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on two, apply, see, specific and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in two, apply, see, specific using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. suggested_title: PAR-25-185 — Pathology: human, including, variety, health
synopsis: This application would address Screening and Functional Validation of Genomic Variants Associated with Human Congenital Anomalies (R01 Clinical Trial Not Allowed) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on human, including, variety, health and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in human, including, variety, health using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

synopsis: This application would address NIH Exploratory/Developmental Research Project Grant (Parent R21 Basic Experimental Studies with Humans Required) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory..

rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts..

rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory.; Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells..

suggested_title: PAR-20-043 — Pathology: laboratory, control, cancer, two
synopsis: This application would address Cancer Center Support Grants (CCSGs) for NCI-designated Cancer Centers (P30 Clinical Trial Optional) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on laboratory, control, cancer, two and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in laboratory, control, cancer, two using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

suggested_title: PAR-20-043 — Pathology: cancer, which, research, support
synopsis: This application would address Cancer Center Support Grants (CCSGs) for NCI-designated Cancer Centers (P30 Clinical Trial Optional) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in cancer, which, research, support using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

synopsis: This application would address SEEDING CRITICAL ADVANCES FOR LEADING ENERGY TECHNOLOGIES WITH UNTAPPED POTENTIAL (SCALEUP) READY by integrating complementary expertise across the team. rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts.; Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in its, apply, end, research using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in tools, program, support, research using the team’s established models and datasets.

Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts..

We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in program, which, research, normal using the team’s established models and datasets.

Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in research, health, studies, have using the team’s established models and datasets.

We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in research, health, approaches, program using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.
